content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Consistency Prompts: Reducing Hallucination by Fact-Space Regularization",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often hallucinate—generating plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content—even when directly prompted for factual accuracy. Existing prompting methods, such as self-consistency or chain-of-thought, provide limited improvements in factuality.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes contexts (medicine, law, research), ensuring factual reliability is paramount. Current advances in prompting focus on reasoning but do not constrain models to the true fact space. Introducing new prompting structures that regularize generation towards factual consistency could shift the paradigm for safe deployment.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual Consistency Prompts (CCP): For a given factual query, generate and append two to three 'counterfactual contrast' prompts—minimally perturbed versions of the query whose answers are known to differ (e.g., changing a date or named entity). The model is instructed to answer all prompts and explicitly highlight distinguishing facts for each, enforcing attention to factual determinants. During inference, output is accepted only if the model’s responses to both factual and counterfactual prompts are mutually consistent (i.e., contradictions are penalized, factual determinants are cited).",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CCP on TruthfulQA, FeQA, and HotpotQA, comparing against vanilla, CoT, and self-consistency prompting. Metrics include factual accuracy (human and automated), hallucination rate, and calibration. Ablations test prompt structure and number of contrasts."
}
